name: Santa Engracia Trail
name_original: Castillete de Pancorbo — circular por el Parque Natural de Montes Obarenes-San Zadornil
description: This difficult 5.9-mile jogging trail in Montes Obarenes-San Zadornil Natural Park offers challenging terrain and scenic views.
region_intro: Jogging around Pancorbo offers a chance to explore a landscape characterized by varied terrain, including hills, valleys, and natural gorges. The region features routes that traverse areas near historical churches and offer views of the surrounding countryside. Many running routes in Pancorbo involve significant elevation changes, providing a challenging experience for runners. Best jogging routes around Pancorbo The most popular jogging route is Santa Engracia Trail, a 5.8 miles (9.4 km) trail that takes about 1 hour 23 minutes to complete. This difficult route features notable elevation gain. Another top favourite among local runners is San Miguel Church loop from Foncea, a difficult 10.1 miles (16.3 km) path. This running route offers views of the landscape around Foncea and the historic church. Local runners also love the Estrechon de Sarlota – Peña Horcada loop from Las Rastrillas, a 12.6 miles (20.3 km) trail leading through varied terrain with significant climbs, often completed in about 3 hours 19 minutes. Jogging around Pancorbo is defined by challenging climbs, scenic loops, and routes near historical landmarks and natural gorges. The network offers options primarily for advanced runners seeking demanding trails. The guide includes highly-rated routes that showcase the best of the region's landscape. More than 140 runners have used komoot to explore Pancorbo's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What is the overall difficulty of running routes around Pancorbo?
A: Jogging in Pancorbo is primarily suited for &lt;strong&gt;advanced runners&lt;/strong&gt; due to the challenging terrain. Most of the 23 available routes feature significant elevation changes, including hills, valleys, and natural gorges, providing a demanding experience. There are only a couple of moderate routes, and no easy options are listed.

Q: Are there any circular running routes in Pancorbo?
A: Yes, many of the running routes around Pancorbo are circular, allowing you to start and finish at the same point. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31456908" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;San Miguel Church loop from Foncea&lt;/a&gt; is a popular difficult 10.1-mile (16.3 km) path that offers scenic views. Another option is the challenging &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31484486" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Estrechon de Sarlota – Peña Horcada loop from Las Rastrillas&lt;/a&gt;, covering 12.6 miles (20.3 km) with significant climbs.

Q: What kind of natural landmarks or viewpoints can I expect to see on a run in Pancorbo?
A: Pancorbo's running trails offer stunning natural landmarks and viewpoints. You can encounter mountain passes like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5580127" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Portillo de Busto&lt;/a&gt; and enjoy panoramic vistas such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5555104" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Views of Valle La Campa and Pancorbo&lt;/a&gt;. The region also features natural monuments like the summit of &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4244321" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Humion&lt;/a&gt; and other scenic overlooks like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4244233" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;TalasDelSomo&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any running routes that pass by historical sites or churches?
A: Absolutely. The region is known for its historical elements. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31456908" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;San Miguel Church loop from Foncea&lt;/a&gt; is a prime example, offering views of the historic church and surrounding landscape. Another route, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31449939" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Church of St. James – View of Pancorbo Gorge loop from Pancorbo&lt;/a&gt;, takes you past the Church of St. James while providing views of the dramatic Pancorbo Gorge.

Q: How many running routes are available in the Pancorbo area?
A: There are over 20 running routes available around Pancorbo on komoot, with a total of 23 documented tours. These routes primarily cater to advanced runners, offering a variety of challenging experiences through the region's diverse terrain.

Q: What is the best time of year to go running in Pancorbo?
A: Pancorbo's varied terrain, with its hills and gorges, can be enjoyed throughout much of the year. Spring and autumn generally offer milder temperatures, which are ideal for tackling the significant elevation changes. Summer can be warm, so early morning or late evening runs are advisable. Winter running is possible, but be prepared for potentially colder conditions and check local weather forecasts, especially at higher elevations.

Q: Are the running trails in Pancorbo suitable for families or beginners?
A: Given the challenging nature of the terrain, with significant climbs and difficult grades, most running trails in Pancorbo are &lt;strong&gt;not ideal for families with young children or absolute beginners&lt;/strong&gt;. The region primarily offers routes for advanced runners seeking demanding trails. There are no easy routes listed, and only a couple of moderate options.

Q: Can I bring my dog on the running routes in Pancorbo?
A: While many outdoor trails in Spain are dog-friendly, it's always recommended to check specific local regulations or signage for each route. Given the rugged and sometimes remote nature of Pancorbo's trails, ensure your dog is fit enough for challenging terrain and long distances. Always keep your dog under control, especially near wildlife or livestock, and carry water for both of you.

Q: Are there options for parking near the jogging trails in Pancorbo?
A: Parking availability can vary depending on the specific trailhead. Many routes, especially those starting from villages like Foncea or Pancorbo itself, will likely have designated parking areas or roadside spots. For routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31446818" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Humión loop from Embalse de Sobrón&lt;/a&gt;, you might find parking near the reservoir. It's advisable to check the route details on komoot for specific parking recommendations before you go.

Q: What do other runners enjoy most about jogging in Pancorbo?
A: Runners frequently praise Pancorbo for its &lt;strong&gt;challenging climbs and scenic loops&lt;/strong&gt;. The komoot community highlights the opportunity to explore varied terrain, including natural gorges and areas near historical landmarks. The demanding nature of the trails is a significant draw for those looking for a rigorous workout amidst beautiful natural surroundings.

Q: Are there any shorter, yet still challenging, running routes available?
A: Yes, if you're looking for a challenging run that's not excessively long, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1050743491/santa-engracia-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Santa Engracia Trail&lt;/a&gt; is a great option. This difficult route is 5.8 miles (9.4 km) long and features notable elevation gain, typically taking about 1 hour 23 minutes to complete. It offers a good balance of intensity and manageable distance.

Q: Are there any places to stop for refreshments or cafes along the running routes?
A: While many of Pancorbo's running routes traverse natural and sometimes remote areas, routes that start or pass through villages like Pancorbo or Foncea may offer opportunities for refreshments. It's always a good idea to carry sufficient water and snacks, especially on longer or more remote trails, as dedicated cafes directly on the trails are uncommon. Check the starting and ending points of your chosen route for nearby amenities.
tips: Curious name for this small top. The view of the lower meadow, full of horses in semi-freedom, impressive

Just above the town of Pancorbo is its old castle which is now in ruins. The path that goes through it crosses what used to be its walls and where the views that fall to the aforementioned town are spectacular.
From this point you can see the whole of Valle La Campa that rises above the town of Pancorbo and that shows us some high mountains that rise in the form of a puncture above the nucleus.

faqs.question: How challenging is the Santa Engracia Trail for jogging?
faqs.answer: This trail is rated as difficult for jogging. It features a 'Red Rate' difficulty for trail running and a 'Black Diamond' for climb difficulty, indicating a strenuous experience. The route also aligns with a T2 Mountain Hiking SAC Scale, requiring sure-footedness and a basic sense of direction.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain can I expect on this route?
faqs.answer: The Santa Engracia Trail is primarily a singletrack. While generally easy to follow, some sections may be somewhat overgrown as it doesn't receive specific mountain bike maintenance. Expect varied terrain typical of a challenging mountain trail.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest along the trail?
faqs.answer: You'll encounter several notable spots. The route passes by Peña del Morete, a prominent summit, and the historic Castillete de Pancorbo. You can also enjoy magnificent Views of Valle La Campa and Pancorbo, especially from the summit areas like Tozal and Ermita de Santa Engracia.

faqs.question: Is the Santa Engracia Trail a loop?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Santa Engracia Trail is designed as a circular route, allowing you to start and finish at the same point without retracing your steps.

faqs.question: Where is the best place to park for the trailhead?
faqs.answer: While specific parking details are not provided, the trail is located in the Montes Obarenes-San Zadornil Natural Park. It's advisable to check local signage or komoot's map for designated parking areas near the starting point, which is typically accessible for this type of circular route.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to jog this trail?
faqs.answer: Given its challenging nature and potential for overgrown sections, spring and autumn generally offer the most pleasant conditions with milder temperatures. Summer can be hot, and winter might present snow or ice, especially at higher elevations, making the already difficult trail even more demanding.

faqs.question: Are dogs allowed on the trail?
faqs.answer: As the trail is within the Montes Obarenes-San Zadornil Natural Park, dogs are generally allowed but must be kept on a leash to protect wildlife and ensure the safety of other trail users. Always practice Leave No Trace principles, including picking up after your pet.

faqs.question: Do I need any permits or pay an entrance fee for the Montes Obarenes-San Zadornil Natural Park?
faqs.answer: Typically, there are no entrance fees or specific permits required for general access to natural parks like Montes Obarenes-San Zadornil for activities such as jogging. However, always check the official park website for any updated regulations or specific rules regarding certain activities or areas within the park.

faqs.question: Does this route connect with other trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Santa Engracia Trail intersects with several other paths. Significant portions of the route follow the Sendero de Santa Engracia (91%) and the Sendero a Peña Morete (15%). You'll also find smaller sections that coincide with the Camino de Santiago Vasco del Interior - Variante de Burgos (9%), Agua y Roca: del Ebro a los Obarenes (8%), and Travesía Santiago (7%).

faqs.question: Is this trail suitable for beginners or families?
faqs.answer: Due to its 'difficult' rating and challenging terrain, this trail is not recommended for beginners or young families. It requires a good level of fitness, sure-footedness, and some experience with mountain trails. More experienced trail runners or hikers seeking a strenuous workout will find it rewarding.

faqs.question: What should I bring for jogging on this trail?
faqs.answer: Given the trail's difficulty and length, it's essential to bring appropriate trail running shoes with good grip, sufficient water, snacks, a map or GPS device (like komoot's app), sun protection, and weather-appropriate clothing. A basic first-aid kit is also advisable.

waypoints.name: Views of Valle La Campa and Pancorbo
